title: Most trading systems don’t fail — they drift
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
A thought I’ve been exploring:

Most trading systems don’t suddenly stop working.

They drift.

Performance degrades slowly:

* signals become less clean
* edge becomes less visible
* results become inconsistent

And it’s hard to tell whether:

* the market has changed
* or the system is being applied outside of its ideal conditions

This seems especially true in environments with changing variance and regime shifts.

So the challenge isn’t just building a good model.

It’s:  
👉 detecting when the model is no longer aligned with the market  
👉 and knowing when to reduce or stop its activity

Curious to hear from people working on systems:

👉 How do you detect drift early?  
👉 Do you rely on performance metrics, regime detection, or something else?

comments.author: BottleInevitable7278
comments.body:
👉 How do you detect drift early? => Performance and risk metrics I look at  
👉 Do you rely on performance metrics, regime detection, or something else?  => yes and for regime I look on VIX levels.

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
That’s a solid combination.

Performance + risk metrics give you a direct view of how the system behaves, and VIX as a regime proxy makes sense to contextualize it.

I think what’s interesting is how early those signals show up. Sometimes performance degradation lags behind the actual regime shift.

Do you ever anticipate it based on volatility expansion (VIX spikes, acceleration), or do you mainly react once your metrics start drifting?

Feels like that timing — early vs reactive — is where a lot of the difference comes from.

replies.author: BottleInevitable7278
replies.body: It really depends on the edge, the market and the timeframe you are trading. There is no one rule for all I would say. The most comming among all is the max. Drawdown in realtime tracking versus on backtested OOS periods, if that realtime exceed 2 times the backtested one, there is something bad going on and you need to dig deeper.
